channel.title: Posts on DAEBFCG
channel.description: Recent content in Posts on DAEBFCG

item.title: Pickup Mounting
item.description:
My first attempt at mounting the pickups utilized #3 screws (humbucker mounting screws) directly into the wood. Machine screws do not hold firmly in wood, especially this small, so this did not work very well. I had planned to inset nuts into the top of the holes for the screws to grab, but insetting nuts this small and flush was not feasible.

The updated solution uses these brass threaded inserts with #4 screws.

The inserts are screwed into 5/16 diameter holes in the floor of the pickup routes.

These provide a solid receptacle to the pickup mounting screws. I am using small lengths of rubber tubing for the “springs” to push the pickups up against the screw pressure, providing height adjustment, and the keep them from bottoming out and crushing the wires.

Getting these inserts positioned precisely is a tedious job. But overall I like this solution. The hold is very solid with no risk of anything coming loose or wearing out.

item.title: New Tone Control Option
item.description:
This introduces another switch for two useful and distinct treble-tone options. The added option bypasses the typical treble-tone signal flow and connects the signal directly to ground through a small capacitor.

This sort of mod is often used on Telecasters. I gave it a try and found it highly effective for a mid-rangey, cutting, almost fuzzy, searing lead tone.

On this guitar, I found the .0015 µF capacitor worked best, which is coincidentally one order of magnitude smaller than the cap I like for the normal tone.

1. signal → 500K Pot → .015 µF capacitor → ground.
2. signal → .0015 µF capacitor → ground.

Alternatively, the switch could be placed after the pot and have it function as a capacitor-selector only, which is often a nice option. This does add the ability to dial in more or less of the effect of the small cap. However the small cap is mostly noticeable when the pot is on 0 (no resistance to ground). So I find it more useful to be able to alternate between the small cap with no resistance and the normal cap with higher/variable resistance just by flipping the switch for a more dramatic change.

Updated Schematic

Added the “TrebleCap” switch and additional capacitor.

Example Recordings

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck tapped), mids 0, bass 10

Original .015 µF cap, treble 10:

New .0015 µF cap direct to ground:

Bridge pickup only (full), bass on 10

Original .015 µF cap, treble 4:

New .0015 µF cap:

A few more for comparison

.015 µF cap

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck tapped), mids 0, bass 10, treble 5:

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck tapped), mids 0, bass 10, treble 3:

.0015 µF cap

Pickups in series (bridge full, neck full), mids 10, bass 0:

item.title: Drawing Schematics
item.description:
My first schematic, made for the guitar circuit.

KiCad Schematic Editor

I am just recently getting comfortable reading and creating schematics. I found a nice oss editor that is part of the KiCad suite of tools.

This program seems good although there is some learning curve. It is helpful to know the keyboard commands for the various tools. The most useful is “A” to place an item. You hit A and are presented with a modal that lets you pick from thousands of various schematic symbols.

To draw a wire between components, you enable the Wire tool by hitting W. It is best to have the cursor ON the pin where you want the wire to start. This is because you do not click down to draw like a typical drawing program, but instead it just starts making a wire as you move the mouse starting with where you were when the tool was enabled.

Escape

Important Tip: Any time you want to get back to the main Select tool, just hit ESC.

Deleting

One annoying aspect is that anytime you click a wire it automatically puts you into Wire tool mode, and is is easy to draw bunch of unwanted wires. Deleting short little wires is harder than it seems like it should be.

Export

Oddly, it does not export to an image file, but instead image data to the clipboard. Not a big deal, just and extra step to create image for use in another program.

item.title: Circuit Test Box
item.description:
New circuit testing rig made from a cigar box.

The inside is fully shielded.

Some mini breadboards for making and connecting modules. The rear-left one is intended to be the main output, and is connected to the output jack in the back. It also has the kill-switch wired to it which comes in handy while arranging connections.

I installed a little handle to lift up the inner lid. I removed it later realizing I can just use one of the attached knobs.

The output jack:

The components are attached from the inside and their wires go up through the holes to be connected to breadboards. I could use some more holes. I made some holes 1/4 inch for the mini switches and some 3/8 for the pots and rotary switches. I probably should have made them all 3/8. Also could use some 1/2 inch holes. Always easier to add more holes and make existing ones bigger.

This also serves as a storage compartment.

It is meant to be able to be closed up, for storage or even more shielding. One design flaw here is that it cannot be fully closed with wires and components sticking up without mashing them down.

My circuit

This is much neater and easier to deal with than the previous modular system. Although, this limits the number of modules to about five or six.

Dial Plates

I like how these dial plates turned out. I am using the set-screw chicken-head knobs for pots that have solid non-splined shafts, and press-on knobs for pots that have the splined shafts. I do like to know what number I am on. Unfortunately set-screws knobs for solid shafts that also have numbers are rare.

The plates for the rotary switches have 12 positions. The plates for the pots go from 0 to 10.

In the image above, the white chicken-head is for selecting from three different capacitors for the main (treble) tone control, and the beige knob adjusts the filtering amount.

item.title: Control Layout
item.description:
Version 1

  1. Pickup Selector toggle switch (neck/series/bridge)
  2. Neck Pickup Tap switch (up: full, down: tapped)
  3. Bridge Pickup Tap switch (up: full, down: tapped)
  4. Direct Out switch (up: direct out, down: normal)
  5. Volume (.75 inch diameter knob)
  6. Treble tone (.5 inch knob)
  7. Middle tone (.5 inch knob)
  8. Bass tone (.5 inch knob)

I like the grid-arrangement of these, although it might be somewhat congested. Ideally the hand would not accidentally hit another control when trying to make an adjustment to one.

I like the direct-out being down at the bottom where it is out of the way, but easy to get to quickly when needed.

Version 2

  1. Pickup tap combo (4-position rotary switch with .5 inch knob)
  2. Treble tone
  3. Middle tone
  4. Bass tone
  5. Volume
  6. Pickup Selector toggle
  7. Direct Out switch

Rotary Option

Since there are four total combinations of pickup-tap selections, I realized the two tap switches could be combined into one 4-way rotary switch.

The combinations are:

  1. Neck Full, Bridge Full
  2. Neck Full, Bridge Tapped
  3. Neck Tapped, Bridge Tapped
  4. Neck Tapped, Bridge Full

Since it is a rotary switch, there is no “stop” so you can get from position 4 back to 1 with one turn.

This is one less hole to drill, cheaper, and likely more reliable long-term. I also find the arrangement can go in a more spaced-out, circular pattern, which is not too bad.

I suspect it would not be as intuitive to use though, compared to two separate up/down switches. The player would need to remember which position on the dial corresponded to the pickup tap combination.

(I considered a more visually-indicative knob for this such as a chicken-head, but decided it would be aesthetically wrong for this guitar.)

Furthermore with either pickup selected by itself, two sequential positions of the knob could possibly have the same result. In other words with one pickup selected it would require either one or two turns to change from tapped to normal depending on the current position. Only when the two pickups are selected together (using the separate toggle switch) would each position on the rotary give a different result.

Bonus Capacitor Selector

Another interesting “twist” to the 4-position rotary switch is that this one is a 3-pole switch. To get the pickup tap combinations I only need to use two of the poles, leaving some extra lugs. One use of these would be to also select different tone capacitors while selecting pickup taps. This is actually pretty cool because I found that with the pickups in tapped-mode I prefer a higher value capacitor. So I could have it where when selecting one of the tapped options, the treble tone control would be through a .022 uf cap, but when selecting one of the full output positions, the treble tone would go through a .015 cap. Or even have four different cap values for each position!

Knobs

I think I have settled on these black, solid aluminum knobs.

.75 inch diameter for the volume. https://www.amplifiedparts.com/products/knob-aluminum-notched-tip-indicator-set-screw-75-diameter

.5 diameter for the rest https://www.amplifiedparts.com/products/knob-aluminum-set-screw-short-line-05-diameter

Switches

I was originally planning to use some mini-toggle switches for the pickup taps and possibly the direct-out. I learned more about these and apparently they are not very durable and tend to break during service. I found some “fat bat” versions on Amazon which seem a bit more solid and have a full 15/32 bushing yet are still in the mini form factor inside. I think they might be ok for the pickup taps.

Another good looking option for the on-on pickup taps would be these non-mini, full size Carling switches. They look great but I think they might be a tight squeeze in the guitar.

That decision on which switches to use for the pickup taps is off the table if the rotary switch is used…

For the pickup selector toggle, I discovered the AxLabs 2-pole/6-lug toggle switch which can indeed be wired as an on-off-on. (And it comes in black!) The on-off-on is needed to get the two pickups in series. This is different from a typical two-pickup selector where the middle position puts the pickups in parallel by being on-on-on. I was thinking I would need to resort to some type of heavy-duty industrial type of on-off-on switch, use a flaky mini-toggle, or modify a standard les-paul type leaf-spring toggle switch to get it to work as on-off-on. This AxLabs switch was a great discovery.

For the direct-out, I like the idea of also using a leaf-spring toggle switch that is easy to flip, as opposed to a short-fat-bat mini toggle or heavy-duty switch. Looking at the AxLabs 6-lug switch, I can take it apart and flip one of the leaves and get it to work as (on)-on-on, meaning it essentially has two positions, ideal for the direct-out switch.

The tip of the direct-out switch will be red.

item.title: Sound Test - With Audio
item.description:
Sound Test

Jump down to the recordings…

Circuit Test Rig

For testing I attached JST-SM connectors to the pickup leads, as well as one from the string-ground/shielding wire. These make convenient and stable test connections - much better than alligator clips.

Recall that these pickup have three wires:

  • full output
  • tapped output
  • ground

Here is a photo of the test rig. It looks like a big mess, and it kind of is.

From the bottom right, and moving counter-clockwise:

1. Input switch box (copper colored square with the white breadboard on top)

The pickups go directly into this. It lets me switch each pickup between the full and tapped output, and also has a three-way switch to select each pickup individually or combine both in series.

2. Neck and Middle Tone (white box, red breadboard)

This consists of a rotary switch for selecting different capacitors and a pot for the amount applied.

This is coming out of the series connection and affects the neck pickup as a tone control, acts like a mid-cut when the pickups are together, and has no effect on the bridge pickup alone. It seems pretty useful in that I can have the neck pickup with a lot of tone applied but then switch to the middle or bridge position and still have a bright sound.

I currently have a .005, .015 and a .022 capacitor in this, and I think I like the .022 here.

1 MG pot, too, so when it is on 10 it has virtually no attenuation.

3. Main output module (large breadboard)

This has the output jack and various pots and such that I am using for bass-cut tone control and the master volume control (the last two components in the circuit).

Volume is a 500k linear pot.

Bass cut is a 1 MG pot with an 1100 picofarad capacitor in parallel with the signal. I tried a few cap values and this one seemed the best.

4. Tone Control Tester (black box)

This is another tone tester with a rotary switch and pot. I am using it for the master tone control, which affects all pickup combinations.

Caps are .015 and .022. I am leaning towards the .015 in this case. I also tried a .044 and as expected did not like the amount of mid range it removed.

500K audio pot.

5. Direct Out Switch (blue breadboard)

This comes next in line after the pickup selector, and allows completely bypassing all the volume and tone controls so the pickups go directly to the jack. I really like this option since it gives such a pure, bright, and LOUD sound at the flick of a switch.

Actually, that neck/middle tone stays in the circuit since it comes in parallel with this switch, so the real direct-out only happens when the bridge pickup is selected by itself, but that is ok.

Sound Samples

Here are some recordings of me noodling around. In each case, I started with the pickup(s) direct out, and then applied various combinations of volume, tone, and tap switching.

Neck Pickup

Pickups Together

Bridge Pickup

These were recorded with no amp, directly into an Audient ID4 audio interface into Ardour, with no signal processing applied at all.

The “reverb” you might hear is just the strings themselves resonating sympathetically which is how I tend to play.

The electric buzzing / static noises are due to all the unshielded wires in the test rig, and should not be present in the completed guitar.

I will record some more tests through an amp soon. And maybe play some actual tunes.

item.title: Pickups Installed
item.description:
Logo removal

I decided to remove the white “Seymour Duncan” logo from the pickups since I do not like any branding on the guitar. Internet lore suggested Magic-Eraser, acetone, alcohol, etc. None of these worked and I had to resort to scraping. This did not turn out as cleanly as I had hoped. It looks fine from six feet away but up close it is apparent that the logo was removed. I am thinking about painting the tops of these with matt black chalkboard paint, but that is a project for another day.

Pickups Installed

Installation was rather tedious. They are held in tight with some rubber tubing but I need to revisit the screw-hole strategy. I was planning to use some small nuts, CA glued into the bottom of the pickup routes, with a hole slightly wider than the screw into the body. I could not quite get this going, and just screwed directly into the wood. I need to rethink this and do it over, but it can be done later as well. Good enough for now.

Next up - sound test!

item.title: Nut and First String Up
item.description:
Nut

Some preliminary work was done on the nut earlier. I am using a dyed black bone nut from StewMac. (I have in the past cut my own from bovine dog-chew bone from the grocery store.)

First sanded to width.

Then the length.

Lastly, the slots are cut and it is shaped up and polished.

First Stringing

The strings are on for the first time! DAddario XL110 for now. These will come off and on a few times during setup and will be replaced with a nicer Pyramid set in the end.

First Impressions: Plays nice and is quite bright (as expected). I played it like this for a couple hours to let it settle and to get a sense of things. Chords sound especially good - very clear. Sound is balanced but definitely leans to the high-end side. Single notes seem fine but will need to be evaluated through pickups and amp to really know.

item.title: Frets Level and Crown
item.description:
Leveling the Frets

The goal is to get all the frets to the exact same height. Mostly they are, but there are some variations due to different wood density along the fretboard and varying installation pressure.

This is crucial to avoid string buzz, misfretting and other playability issues.

With the truss rod adjusted so the fretboard is perfectly flat, a file is run over them to remove any high spots. Each fret has a line of Sharpie marker across it to identify which frets are getting hit. Once all the marker lines are rubbed off they are all the same height.

Fortunately there is not much work needed since I was careful during fret installation.

Then each fret is checked with a rocker (card scraper) to check its height relative to its two neighbors. If it rocks, it is high.

Any high spots are fine tuned with a small file.

Crowning

Now the frets are all the same height, but flat on top. Time to recreate the rounded top (crown) across them.

This is done with a concave-shaped crowning file (medium width burr).

Polish

Once the crowns are reestablished it is time to sand out all the file marks and polish up the frets. I start with 320 grit. Then down to 600, 1,000, and all the way to 12,000. 1,500 through 12,000 are done with micro mesh polishing pads.

Finally I go over them with a buffing wheel on the dremel.

This process gets them to a mirror like finish. The photo does not do them justice, but they are really slick and should play nicely.

The black residue from the metal polishing will come right off with some naptha.

Stainless Steel Frets

This is my first time using stainless steel frets. I was a bit worried since they are much harder than standard nickel-silver and allegedly much more difficult to work and rough on the tools. I did not really find this to be the case. They are harder and take a few more file strokes and sanding passes to remove material, but it is not a big deal at all.

Supposedly they do not wear out.

I like them.

item.title: Fretting
item.description:
Fret Preparation

First we make each fret.

This consists of:

  1. Bending to the correct radius
  2. Cutting each fret to the correct length
  3. Removing the end of the tang that overlaps the fretboard binding
  4. Pre-dressing the ends

I am using pre-bent fretwire, so step 1 can be skipped. I have used fretwire from 2 foot straight lengths as well as long coiled pieces, and getting it pre-radiused is the way to go. This is from Philadelphia Luthier Tools who sells packs of 25 pre-bent to a 12 inch radius for close to the same cost as buying in bulk.

This guitar is getting 47x104 Jescar stainless steel.

Cut to length and tang removal

This is easily done with a Dremel and metal-cutting disk.

Each fret is cut to length and de-tanged. Some light filing on the bottom where the tang was removed is usually needed.

The prepared frets are kept organized in a holder.

End Dressing

I pre-dress the ends of my frets before installing.

First I pre-file each corner to a 45 degree angle, then come in over the top with the fret-crowning file also at a 45 degree angle. This is just to give the next step a head-start.

Then the semi-hemispherical shape is ground smooth on a special emery polishing wheel in the Dremel. This concave-edged wheel was made by running it over a small round file.

Just a couple seconds in this and the fret ends come out remarkably round and smooth with no rough edges at all.

Installation

With the frets prepared to this extent, installation is a breeze.

First a bead of hide glue is spread into the fret slot. Then wiped off flush with DRY paper towel.

(Notice how I accidentally skipped a fret trying to get a photo of the process…)

The fret is then positioned and lightly pressed (not pounded) in with a soft mallet just enough to get it to stay put.

The fret is seated home using a fret-press caul mounted in the drill press.

Lastly, the excess glue is wiped off with a warm wet rag.

Fretboard finishing

I let the hide glue dry about four hours and lightly sand the fretboard between the frets to 600 (to knock back the raised grain from being wetted) , then get the first coat of tung oil on the fretboard. This stuff takes several days to cure between coats.

The first coat is a 50/50 mix of pure tung oil and food-grade D-limonene. The thinning this way helps the first coat penetrate much better (and dry faster). The second coat will be straight tung oil.

Before:

After:

At this stage, the frets are sort of rough looking from the shaping and installation process. These will shine up nicely once the leveling and crowning is done later.
